citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
Two dogs have been identified at the facility as requiring veterinary care. The facility did not identify the veterinary issues
prior to the inspection.
A female king charles spaniel (microchip# 1170) has excessive brown tarter buildup on the top teeth near the gumline,
and the gums are red and inflamed. There is a loose tooth on the upper jaw of the dog that is mobile with minimal
pressure applied to the gum. During the examination the tooth was visibly mobile when the dog would move the tongue
across the teeth. The dog was reacting to the examination by pulling its head away from the inspector and licensee.
A male mini poodle (microchip# 3951) had severe gum recession on the upper jaw. While performing the physical
examination of the dog the inspector observed movement in the front upper teeth when minimal pressure was applied to
the gum. The front teeth also had hard light brown buildup between them. The dog was reacting to the exam pulling the
head away and snapping the mouth shut.
Failure to conduct adequate daily observations to assess the health and well-being of the dogs with direct and frequent
communication to the attending veterinarian, lead to a delay in treatment of the dental issues and cause difficulty eating,
pain, and suffering.
Correct by ensuring that the animals listed above, and the condition of the animal are reported to the attending
veterinarian so that the animals can be properly examined, diagnosed, and treated. Additionally, ensure that daily
observation of animals is adequate to identify any signs of poor health. Ensure that there is accurate and timely
communication of problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being conveyed to the attending veterinarian. Correct by,
5:00 PM on 16-April-2026.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
There is an excessive accumulation of excreta in the outdoor runs of approximately 9 adult dog enclosures. The licensee
stated that the outdoor runs are spot cleaned every three or so days. The outdoor runs were last spot cleaned the week
prior on 09-April-2026.
Failure to provide adequate cleaning and sanitization in the primary enclosures increases the risk of disease transmission,
attracts insects/pests, and increases odors. Excreta could potentially cause soiling on the bodies of the dogs being
housed in the affected enclosures.
The licensee must remove excreta and food waste from the primary enclosure daily and from under the primary
enclosures as often as necessary to prevent an excessive accumulation of feces and food waste, to prevent soiling of the
dogs contained in the primary enclosures, and to reduce disease hazards, insects, pests and odors. Correct by 22-April-
2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the Licensee.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3)  Direct
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
Two dogs have been identified at the facility as requiring veterinary care. The facility did not identify the veterinary issues 
prior to the inspection. 
 
A female king charles spaniel (microchip# 1170) has excessive brown tarter buildup on the top teeth near the gumline, 
and the gums are red and inflamed. There is a loose tooth on the upper jaw of the dog that is mobile with minimal 
pressure applied to the gum. During the examination the tooth was visibly mobile when the dog would move the tongue 
across the teeth. The dog was reacting to the examination by pulling its head away from the inspector and licensee.  
 
A male mini poodle (microchip# 3951) had severe gum recession on the upper jaw. While performing the physical 
examination of the dog the inspector observed movement in the front upper teeth when minimal pressure was applied to 
the gum. The front teeth also had hard light brown buildup between them. The dog was reacting to the exam pulling the 
head away and snapping the mouth shut.  
 
Failure to conduct adequate daily observations to assess the health and well-being of the dogs with direct and frequent 
communication to the attending veterinarian, lead to a delay in treatment of the dental issues and cause difficulty eating, 
pain, and suffering. 
 
Correct by ensuring that the animals listed above, and the condition of the animal are reported to the attending 
veterinarian so that the animals can be properly examined, diagnosed, and treated. Additionally, ensure that daily 
observation of animals is adequate to identify any signs of poor health. Ensure that there is accurate and timely 
communication of problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being conveyed to the attending veterinarian. Correct by, 
5:00 PM on 16-April-2026. 
3.11(a) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
There is an excessive accumulation of excreta in the outdoor runs of approximately 9 adult dog enclosures. The licensee 
stated that the outdoor runs are spot cleaned every three or so days. The outdoor runs were last spot cleaned the week 
prior on 09-April-2026.

Failure to provide adequate cleaning and sanitization in the primary enclosures increases the risk of disease transmission, 
attracts insects/pests, and increases odors. Excreta could potentially cause soiling on the bodies of the dogs being 
housed in the affected enclosures. 
 
The licensee must remove excreta and food waste from the primary enclosure daily and from under the primary 
enclosures as often as necessary to prevent an excessive accumulation of feces and food waste, to prevent soiling of the 
dogs contained in the primary enclosures, and to reduce disease hazards, insects, pests and odors. Correct by 22-April-
2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the Licensee.
